question: Show that the set S defined by 1 ∈ S and s + t ∈ 𐐠 whenever s ∈ S and t ∈ S is the set of positive integers.
response: "You’re essentially given a **recursive definition** of a set ( S ):* Base rule: ( 1 \in S )* Closure rule: if ( s \in S ) and ( t \in S ), then ( s + t \in S )We want to show that **( S ) is exactly the set of positive integers** ( \mathbb{Z}^+ = {1,2,3,\dots} ).---## Step 1: Show ( S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+ )This direction is straightforward.* The base element ( 1 ) is a positive integer.* The sum of two positive integers is also a positive integer.So every element generated using the rules stays positive.Thus, every element of ( S ) is a positive integer:[S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+]---## Step 2: Show ( \mathbb{Z}^+ \subseteq S )Now we prove that **every positive integer is in ( S )**.We use **mathematical induction**.### Base case:( 1 \in S ) (given)### Inductive step:Assume ( n \in S ) for some positive integer ( n ).We want to show ( n+1 \in S ).* We already know ( 1 \in S )* By the closure rule:  [  n + 1 \in S  ]So if ( n \in S ), then ( n+1 \in S ).---By induction, **all positive integers are in ( S )**:[\mathbb{Z}^+ \subseteq S]---## ConclusionWe have shown both:[S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+ \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbb{Z}^+ \subseteq S]Therefore:[S = \mathbb{Z}^+]---## IntuitionStarting from 1 and repeatedly adding elements of the set to each other, you can build:* ( 2 = 1+1 )* ( 3 = 2+1 )* ( 4 = 3+1 ), etc.So you generate **all positive integers—and nothing else**.
